topic: A college student discovers their roommate has been reading their journal
raw_output:
[story 1]

The smell of old paper and stale coffee hung heavy in the air of the cramped dorm room. I found the evidence tucked beneath my desk chair—my private thoughts, meticulously transcribed, but rearranged. My stomach clenched, a tight knot forming beneath my ribs. I stared at the page, the ink seeming to mock my attempt to ignore it. When my roommate, Alex, walked in, whistling a tuneless melody, I kept my gaze fixed on my textbook, forcing a neutral expression onto my face. "Everything okay?" Alex asked, leaning against the doorframe, a casual air about them. I cleared my throat, my voice sounding unnaturally high. "Yeah, fine. Just studying." I didn't look up, my fingers gripping the edge of the desk until my knuckles turned white. The silence stretched, thick and suffocating, as Alex finally moved toward their own side of the room, their movements deliberately slow.

[story 2]

I felt a hot flush creep up my neck as I realized the discrepancy in my notebook. My handwriting, my secret language, was scattered across the shared space, clearly belonging to someone else. A deep, simmering resentment began to bubble up, making my jaw tighten. I watched Alex across the room, their attention fixed on a video game, completely oblivious to the silent invasion. I wanted to confront them, to demand an explanation, but the words caught in my throat, tasting like ash. Instead, I started tidying my own side of the room with aggressive, jerky movements, stacking books with unnecessary force. When Alex finally paused their game to stretch, I met their eyes briefly. My expression was carefully blank, a mask of detached indifference, but the tension radiating off me was palpable. I just needed them to understand, without me having to say a single word.

[story 3]

It was the way the margin notes were written—a specific shade of blue ink I hadn't used—that finally cracked my composure. A slow burn ignited in my chest, a refusal to simply let this pass without acknowledgment. I snatched the journal from the nightstand, my heart hammering against my ribs like a trapped bird. I paced the small space, my movements sharp and agitated, refusing to settle into any semblance of calm. When Alex entered the room, carrying a laundry basket, I didn't hide the book. I held it up, my voice low and steady, though the tremor in my hands betrayed the internal turmoil. "What is this?" I asked, the question edged with a brittle sharpness. Alex froze, the basket halfway to the closet. The air crackled with unspoken accusation, and I held their gaze, daring them to look away.
